parts.text: Can we move over seamlessly to https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim so we can use vim capabiltiies fully with opencode

I’ll first inspect the existing OpenCode/Neovim integration points and the `opencode-vim` installation requirements, then make the smallest dotfiles-side changes needed without touching the separate Neovim repo unless it’s actually required.

# OpenCode Vim

[](#opencode-vim)

[![npm version](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/57bd5ba12fc35c2e3cc8ef5c6204a0250754da3d5cd505c6437745cbfa1564bd/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f6e706d2f762f406c656f68656e6f6e2f6f63763f7374796c653d666c61742d737175617265266c6f676f3d6e706d266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@leohenon/ocv) [![CI](https://camo.githubusercontent.)](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim/actions/workflows/ci.yml) [![Last commit](https://camo.githubusercontent.)](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim/commits/ocv) [![Bun](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/99527467af2abb696ccdaeb6826bbae9d8a6ee9edbdc392f9cd646b5d874b45d/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f72756e74696d652d42756e2d6639663165313f7374796c653d666c61742d737175617265266c6f676f3d62756e266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465)](https://bun.sh)

opencode fork with vim mode. Syncs with upstream releases.

[![](/leohenon/opencode-vim/raw/ocv/.github/demo.gif)](/leohenon/opencode-vim/blob/ocv/.github/demo.gif)

## Install

[](#install)

### npm

[](#npm)

npm i -g @leohenon/ocv

### Homebrew

[](#homebrew)

brew install leohenon/tap/ocv

### curl

[](#curl)

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/leohenon/opencode-vim/ocv/install.sh | sudo sh

Then run:

ocv

## Update

[](#update)

npm i -g @leohenon/ocv@latest
# or
brew upgrade ocv
# or
ocv update

## Features

[](#features)

### Vim motions

[](#vim-motions)

Toggle via command palette (`Ctrl+p` -> `Toggle vim mode`).

**Movement**

`h` `j` `k` `l` `w` `b` `e` `W` `B` `E` `0` `^` `_` `$` `{` `}` `gg` `G` `f` `F` `t` `T` `;` `,` `Ctrl+e` `Ctrl+y` `Ctrl+d` `Ctrl+u` `Ctrl+f` `Ctrl+b`

Note

Unicode word boundaries are not supported.

**Editing**

`i` `I` `a` `A` `o` `O` `R` `x` `~` `dd` `dw` `db` `d}` `d{` `cc` `cw` `cb` `C` `c}` `c{` `S` `J`

**yank / put / undo**

`yy` `yw` `y}` `y{` `p` `P` `u` `ctrl+r`

-   Copy the current prompt selection with `<leader>y` (default: `ctrl+x` then `y`).
-   Configure it with `keybinds.prompt_copy_selection`.
-   If a prompt selection exists, `<leader>y` copies it. Otherwise it keeps the existing message copy behavior.
-   To sync yanks and pastes with the system clipboard, see [System clipboard register](#system-clipboard-register).

**Visual**

`v` `V`

### Anthropic OAuth

[](#anthropic-oauth)

Claude subscriptions built-in with `/connect`. No plugins or configuration needed.

### Copy Mode

[](#copy-mode)

Text selection from the chat session view.

Works similarly to tmux copy mode within opencode tui.

[![](/leohenon/opencode-vim/raw/ocv/.github/demo-copy-mode.gif)](/leohenon/opencode-vim/blob/ocv/.github/demo-copy-mode.gif)

-   Enter copy mode with `<leader>v`.
-   Navigate with `h` `j` `k` `l` or arrow keys (`Left` `Down` `Up` `Right`).
-   Press `v` / `V` to start character-wise or line-wise selection.
-   `y` yanks to the vim register.
-   `Enter` copies to the system clipboard.
-   `Escape` exits visual mode, `q` exits copy mode.
-   `z` `zt` `zz` `zb` adjust copy-mode scroll positioning.
-   `H` / `M` / `L` jump to the top / middle / bottom of the viewport.

Tip

Configure the entry key with `keybinds.copy_mode` in your config if you want something other than `<leader>v`.

Note

Copy mode collapses code diffs into a single column for easy copying.

[![](/leohenon/opencode-vim/raw/ocv/.github/copy-demo.gif)](/leohenon/opencode-vim/blob/ocv/.github/copy-demo.gif)

### Prompt Input

[](#prompt-input)

Prompt input height is configurable with `prompt_max_height` in `tui.json`.

When the prompt grows past the visible area, a scrollbar appears automatically.

{
  "prompt\_max\_height": 35,
  "prompt\_scrollbar": true
}

[![](/leohenon/opencode-vim/raw/ocv/.github/scrollbar.gif)](/leohenon/opencode-vim/blob/ocv/.github/scrollbar.gif)

Note

When typing `gg` / `G` focus the prompt input.

Warning

Setting `prompt_max_height` above `40` is not recommended.

### Minimal UI

[](#minimal-ui)

Hides extra UI hints and tips.

Default

Minimal

[![](/leohenon/opencode-vim/raw/ocv/.github/minimal-ui-off.png)](/leohenon/opencode-vim/blob/ocv/.github/minimal-ui-off.png)

[![](/leohenon/opencode-vim/raw/ocv/.github/minimal-ui-on.png)](/leohenon/opencode-vim/blob/ocv/.github/minimal-ui-on.png)

Toggle via command palette (`Ctrl+p` -> `Toggle minimal ui`).

## Configuration

[](#configuration)

### Submit behavior

[](#submit-behavior)

By default, vim insert mode keeps `Enter` for newlines and normal mode uses `Enter` to submit. If you want `Enter` to submit from insert mode too, add this to `tui.json`:

{
  "vim\_enter\_submit": true
}

When `vim_enter_submit` is enabled, line returns are still available through `input_newline`.

{
  "keybinds": {
    "input\_newline": "alt+return"
  }
}

If you keep `vim_enter_submit` disabled but want a separate submit key that works from insert mode, configure `input_force_submit`:

{
  "keybinds": {
    "input\_force\_submit": "alt+return"
  }
}

By default, `input_force_submit` is unbound.

### System clipboard register

[](#system-clipboard-register)

By default, vim mode uses an internal register for `y` and `p`. If you want yank and paste to use the system clipboard instead, add this to `tui.json`:

{
  "vim\_system\_clipboard\_register": true
}

With this enabled, yank operations sync to the system clipboard and `p` / `P` paste from it.

Note

Terminal/OS clipboard shortcuts don’t preserve Vim linewise register state. External clipboard text is pasted as characterwise text.

## Feedback

[](#feedback)

Have a suggestion? [Open an issue](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim/issues).

## About

The open source coding agent with vim mode.

[opencode.ai](https://opencode.ai "https://opencode.ai")

parts.text: `opencode-vim` is a forked OpenCode binary exposed as `ocv`, not a Vim/Neovim plugin. I’m going to wire it in alongside the existing `opencode` setup first so the migration is reversible and doesn’t break current OpenCode plugins/config.

state.output:
<path>/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-nvim.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # opencode.nvim vs tmux Side Pane
2: 
3: OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
4: 
5: ## Why the plugin is a material upgrade
6: 
7: - **Zero-copy context** – prompts can reference placeholders such as `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, or `@visible`. The plugin resolves those against your current buffer/selection before sending them to OpenCode, so you stop yanking snippets into the side pane.
8: - **Prompt + operator ergonomics** – `require("opencode").ask`, `select`, and the operator wrapper let you reuse visual selections, ranges, and dot-repeat. Side panes are strictly linear terminals with no operator integration.
9: - **Editor-driven review loop** – when OpenCode proposes edits, the plugin opens a tab with `:diffpatch`, lets you accept/reject per hunk (`dp`, `do`, `]c`, `[c`), and reloads buffers on approval. The side pane requires manual patch application or copy/paste.
10: - **Permission + event awareness** – server-sent events surface inside Neovim via the `OpencodeEvent` autocmd, so you can hook custom automation (notifications, statusline, etc.). The pane view only shows whatever the TUI prints.
11: - **Experimental LSP bridge** – enabling `vim.g.opencode_opts.lsp.enabled` turns hover/code-action requests into OpenCode prompts. There is no equivalent API from the tmux pane.
12: - **Session UX** – the plugin auto-starts/stops OpenCode servers, exposes commands (`session.select`, `session.interrupt`, etc.) through pickers, and includes optional Snacks integrations. Launching a pane simply spawns `opencode` and leaves lifecycle management to you.
13: 
14: ## What stays the same
15: 
16: - The backend assistant is still OpenCode, so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
17: - You can connect the plugin to any running OpenCode instance via the `server.port` option, which means the tmux binding can keep a long-lived side pane if you want a dedicated transcript while Neovim handles buffer-aware asks.
18: - Permissions and hooks still flow through the existing `.opencode/plugins/claude-compat.ts` stack—`opencode.nvim` just surfaces them in-editor.
19: 
20: ## Comparison snapshot
21: 
22: | Capability | tmux side pane | `opencode.nvim` |
23: |------------|----------------|-----------------|
24: | Context capture | Manual copy/paste | Automatic via placeholders (`@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, etc.) |
25: | Prompt ergonomics | TUI input only | Lua API + Snacks picker/input + operator support + dot-repeat |
26: | Edit review | Copy/paste or apply patch manually | Built-in diff tab with accept/reject bindings (`da`, `dr`, `dp`, `do`) |
27: | Permission handling | Raw JSON/log output | Inline prompts after idle + autocmd hooks |
28: | Session control | `opencode` CLI shortcuts | `require("opencode").command("session.*")` + configurable keymaps |
29: | LSP integration | None | Optional hover/code-action bridge |
30: | Statusline signal | Pane title only | `require("opencode").statusline` component |
31: 
32: ## Adoption guidance
33: 
34: 1. Install the plugin via LazyVim (see upstream README snippet) and set `vim.g.opencode_opts.server.port` to match the port our scripts already use (or let it spawn its own terminal).
35: 2. Keep `vim.o.autoread = true` so edits from OpenCode reload correctly.
36: 3. Reuse the recommended keymaps (`<C-a>` ask, `<C-x>` select, `go` operator) or map them under `<leader>o` to avoid conflicts with tmux bindings.
37: 4. Run `:checkhealth opencode` after wiring it up; the check validates that OpenCode is discoverable and events are flowing.
38: 5. Continue to use the tmux pane when you need a dedicated transcript view or when Neovim is not open—both entry points can coexist because they talk to the same OpenCode backend.
39: 
40: By moving repetitive prompt + context work into Neovim we remove most of the friction highlighted in this ticket while leaving the tmux bindings available for workflows outside the editor.
41: 
42: ## Hybrid workflow with CodeCompanion
43: 
44: `codecompanion.nvim` stays in the config as the “chat + workflow” orchestrator while `opencode.nvim` owns buffer-aware operators and edit review. The shared adapter stack looks like this:
45: 
46: - `codecompanion.nvim` adapters target OpenCode via the built-in ACP helper, so you can open a standalone chat buffer, trigger slash commands (e.g., `/review`, `/explain`, `/diff`), or launch workflows that gather buffers/diagnostics before sending them through OpenCode.
47: - `opencode.nvim` handles inline asks (`ask`, `select`, `operator`) plus the diff-based permission workflow. Both plugins can point to the same running OpenCode instance by sharing a port in `vim.g.opencode_opts.server` and the CodeCompanion adapter config.
48: - Keymap coexistence: keep the CodeCompanion palette on `<leader>a*` (multi-buffer chat) and reserve `<C-a>/<C-x>/go` for `opencode.nvim`. The two layers stay orthogonal.
49: 
50: ### Reference Lua snippets
51: 
52: ```lua
53: -- lazy.nvim spec excerpt (~/neovim/lua/plugins/opencode.lua)
54: return {
55:   "nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim",
56:   dependencies = { "folke/snacks.nvim" },
57:   config = function()
58:     vim.g.opencode_opts = {
59:       server = {
60:         port = tonumber(vim.env.OPENCODE_PORT or 3333),
61:       },
62:       prompts = {
63:         diagnostics = { name = "diagnostics", prompt = "Explain @diagnostics" },
64:         refactor = { name = "refactor", prompt = "Refactor @this for readability" },
65:       },
66:     }
67:     vim.keymap.set({ "n", "x" }, "<C-a>", function()
68:       require("opencode").ask("@this: ", { submit = true })
69:     end, { desc = "Ask OpenCode" })
70:     vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("User", {
71:       pattern = "OpencodeEvent:*",
72:       callback = function(args)
73:         require("opencode.status").update(args.data.event)
74:       end,
75:     })
76:   end,
77: }
78: ```
79: 
80: ```lua
81: -- CodeCompanion adapter excerpt (~/neovim/lua/plugins/codecompanion.lua)
82: return {
83:   "olimorris/codecompanion.nvim",
84:   opts = {
85:     adapters = {
86:       opencode = function()
87:         return require("codecompanion.adapters").extend("opencode", {
88:           schema = { model = { default = "openai/gpt-5.4" } },
89:         })
90:       end,
91:     },
92:     strategies = {
93:       chat = { adapter = "opencode" },
94:       inline = { adapter = "opencode" },
95:     },
96:     slash_commands = {
97:       diff = {
98:         opts = {
99:           prompt = "Review the following git diff for correctness and readability: @diff",
100:         },
101:       },
102:     },
103:   },
104: }
105: ```
106: 
107: With this split you can define explicit workflows:
108: 
109: - **Diagnostics review** – `:CodeCompanion /diagnostics` to summarize errors, then `:lua require("opencode").prompt("fix")` for the focused buffer patch.
110: - **Refactor selection** – visual select code, hit `go` to enqueue via the operator, then accept/reject hunks in the diff tab.
111: - **Diff review** – `CodeCompanion` slash command builds high-level TODOs, while `opencode.nvim`’s `prompt("diff")` opens the same git diff with accept/reject controls.
112: 
113: ## SSE logging + Entire checkpoints
114: 
115: - `.opencode/plugins/sse-recorder.ts` listens to every OpenCode SSE event, writes a JSONL audit log under `.entire/opencode/sse/events.jsonl`, and mirrors summary payloads into `entire hooks opencode sse-event` so checkpoints capture turn-by-turn metadata.
116: - Whenever an event contains a diff/patch payload, the plugin stores a timestamped snapshot inside `.entire/opencode/sse/diffs/` and emits a secondary `sse-diff` hook with the file path.
117: - The harness `scripts/opencode/test-sse-recorder.ts` exercises the plugin in isolation to guarantee log + diff files are produced.
118: - Use `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh --cat` (or `--meta`) to inspect the newest AI patch. Diffview can read those patch files through its existing tmux/Zsh discovery hooks, so you can replay AI-generated edits even after closing the OpenCode TUI.
119: 
120: ## Diffview integration pointers
121: 
122: 1. Keep `.config/fish/conf.d/diffview-follow.fish` enabled so every `cd` notifies Neovim’s Diffview window about repo changes.
123: 2. When the SSE recorder drops a new patch, run `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh` to get the file path, then inside Neovim run `:DiffviewFileHistory <patch>` or open it in a scratch buffer for review.
124: 3. Because the recorder stores metadata (`*.patch.json`), you can show the originating session/message in statuslines or in Entire checkpoints.
125: 
126: ## Automated validation
127: 
128: `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` now covers the new surfaces:
129: 
130: - `scripts/opencode/test-sse-recorder.ts` (Bun harness) ensures the recorder plugin logs events/diffs.
131: - `scripts/opencode/test-nvim-health.sh` runs `nvim --headless` → `luafile` (module probes) → `:checkhealth opencode` → `:checkhealth codecompanion` so CI flags missing plugins immediately. Set `OPENCODE_NVIM_APPNAME` if you use a non-default Neovim profile.
132: - `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh` gives tooling + tests a stable entry point for the most recent diff snapshot.
133: 
134: Together these cover the “hybrid UI + SSE logging + Diffview replay” workflow discussed in the ticket.
135: 
136: ## Wrapped.nvim dashboard
137: 
138: - `aikhe/wrapped.nvim` is installed (Lazy spec in `~/neovim/lua/plugins/wrapped.lua`) with `nvzone/volt` so you can launch a dashboard of repo activity via `:WrappedNvim` or the shortcut `<leader>aw`.
139: - The dashboard pulls git stats from `vim.fn.stdpath("config")` (override with `NVIM_WRAPPED_PATH`) and renders commit heatmaps, plugin growth charts, and large-file summaries. Size/border are tuned for tmux panes via rounded border + 75% of current editor dimensions.
140: - Use `<` / `>` to cycle years, `r` to refresh after new commits, and `q` to close. Because data collection is async, a loading screen appears until git/file/plugin tasks finish.
141: - Pairing this dashboard with the SSE recorder means Entire checkpoints capture both AI session history and evolving Neovim configuration history (for example, referencing the most active month when reviewing opencode usage).

(End of file - total 141 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
# opencode.nvim vs tmux Side Pane

OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.

## Why the plugin is a material upgrade

- **Zero-copy context** – prompts can reference placeholders such as `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, or `@visible`. The plugin resolves those against your current buffer/selection before sending them to OpenCode, so you stop yanking snippets into the side pane.
- **Prompt + operator ergonomics** – `require("opencode").ask`, `select`, and the operator wrapper let you reuse visual selections, ranges, and dot-repeat. Side panes are strictly linear terminals with no operator integration.
- **Editor-driven review loop** – when OpenCode proposes edits, the plugin opens a tab with `:diffpatch`, lets you accept/reject per hunk (`dp`, `do`, `]c`, `[c`), and reloads buffers on approval. The side pane requires manual patch application or copy/paste.
- **Permission + event awareness** – server-sent events surface inside Neovim via the `OpencodeEvent` autocmd, so you can hook custom automation (notifications, statusline, etc.). The pane view only shows whatever the TUI prints.
- **Experimental LSP bridge** – enabling `vim.g.opencode_opts.lsp.enabled` turns hover/code-action requests into OpenCode prompts. There is no equivalent API from the tmux pane.
- **Session UX** – the plugin auto-starts/stops OpenCode servers, exposes commands (`session.select`, `session.interrupt`, etc.) through pickers, and includes optional Snacks integrations. Launching a pane simply spawns `opencode` and leaves lifecycle management to you.

## What stays the same

- The backend assistant is still OpenCode, so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
- You can connect the plugin to any running OpenCode instance via the `server.port` option, which means the tmux binding can keep a long-lived side pane if you want a dedicated transcript while Neovim handles buffer-aware asks.
- Permissions and hooks still flow through the existing `.opencode/plugins/claude-compat.ts` stack—`opencode.nvim` just surfaces them in-editor.

## Comparison snapshot

# Scripts Index

112 scripts and 20 subdirectories supporting the dotfiles ecosystem. Scripts use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` with `set -euo pipefail`.

## Key Entry Points

| Script | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `setup.sh` | Main machine bootstrap (Homebrew, stow, tools, configs) |
| `setup-mobile-coding.sh` | Mosh + Tailscale for remote access |
| `setup-selfhost-llm.sh` | Ollama + Open WebUI local LLM |

| `tmux/` | tmux watcher, session management |
| `pihole/` | Pi-hole DNS management |
| `ticket-queue/` | Ticket queue management |
| `tools/` | Standalone tool scripts |

## Conventions

- Bash scripts: `set -euo pipefail`, quote all variables, snake_case functions
- File naming: kebab-case (e.g., `setup-mobile-coding.sh`)
- Shared code: import from `lib/` directory
- Never use `((var++))` with `set -e` — use `var=$((var + 1))` instead

</system-reminder>

function opencode-doctor --description "Run OpenCode project preflight checks"
    set -l script "$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/opencode/doctor.sh"

    if not test -x "$script"
        echo "Error: OpenCode doctor script not found: $script" >&2
        return 1
    end

    $script $argv
end

/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-nvim.md:
  Line 1: # opencode.nvim vs tmux Side Pane

  Line 3: OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.

  Line 8: - **Prompt + operator ergonomics** – `require("opencode").ask`, `select`, and the operator wrapper let you reuse visual selections, ranges, and dot-repeat. Side panes are strictly linear terminals with no operator integration.

  Line 11: - **Experimental LSP bridge** – enabling `vim.g.opencode_opts.lsp.enabled` turns hover/code-action requests into OpenCode prompts. There is no equivalent API from the tmux pane.

  Line 12: - **Session UX** – the plugin auto-starts/stops OpenCode servers, exposes commands (`session.select`, `session.interrupt`, etc.) through pickers, and includes optional Snacks integrations. Launching a pane simply spawns `opencode` and leaves lifecycle management to you.

  Line 16: - The backend assistant is still OpenCode, so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.

  Line 18: - Permissions and hooks still flow through the existing `.opencode/plugins/claude-compat.ts` stack—`opencode.nvim` just surfaces them in-editor.

  Line 22: | Capability | tmux side pane | `opencode.nvim` |

  Line 28: | Session control | `opencode` CLI shortcuts | `require("opencode").command("session.*")` + configurable keymaps |

  Line 30: | Statusline signal | Pane title only | `require("opencode").statusline` component |

  Line 34: 1. Install the plugin via LazyVim (see upstream README snippet) and set `vim.g.opencode_opts.server.port` to match the port our scripts already use (or let it spawn its own terminal).

  Line 37: 4. Run `:checkhealth opencode` after wiring it up; the check validates that OpenCode is discoverable and events are flowing.

  Line 44: `codecompanion.nvim` stays in the config as the “chat + workflow” orchestrator while `opencode.nvim` owns buffer-aware operators and edit review. The shared adapter stack looks like this:

  Line 47: - `opencode.nvim` handles inline asks (`ask`, `select`, `operator`) plus the diff-based permission workflow. Both plugins can point to the same running OpenCode instance by sharing a port in `vim.g.opencode_opts.server` and the CodeCompanion adapter config.

  Line 48: - Keymap coexistence: keep the CodeCompanion palette on `<leader>a*` (multi-buffer chat) and reserve `<C-a>/<C-x>/go` for `opencode.nvim`. The two layers stay orthogonal.

  Line 53: -- lazy.nvim spec excerpt (~/neovim/lua/plugins/opencode.lua)

  Line 55:   "nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim",

  Line 58:     vim.g.opencode_opts = {

  Line 68:       require("opencode").ask("@this: ", { submit = true })

  Line 73:         require("opencode.status").update(args.data.event)

  Line 86:       opencode = function()

  Line 87:         return require("codecompanion.adapters").extend("opencode", {

  Line 93:       chat = { adapter = "opencode" },

  Line 94:       inline = { adapter = "opencode" },

  Line 109: - **Diagnostics review** – `:CodeCompanion /diagnostics` to summarize errors, then `:lua require("opencode").prompt("fix")` for the focused buffer patch.

  Line 111: - **Diff review** – `CodeCompanion` slash command builds high-level TODOs, while `opencode.nvim`’s `prompt("diff")` opens the same git diff with accept/reject controls.

  Line 115: - `.opencode/plugins/sse-recorder.ts` listens to every OpenCode SSE event, writes a JSONL audit log under `.entire/opencode/sse/events.jsonl`, and mirrors summary payloads into `entire hooks opencode sse-event` so checkpoints capture turn-by-turn metadata.

  Line 116: - Whenever an event contains a diff/patch payload, the plugin stores a timestamped snapshot inside `.entire/opencode/sse/diffs/` and emits a secondary `sse-diff` hook with the file path.

  Line 117: - The harness `scripts/opencode/test-sse-recorder.ts` exercises the plugin in isolation to guarantee log + diff files are produced.

  Line 118: - Use `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh --cat` (or `--meta`) to inspect the newest AI patch. Diffview can read those patch files through its existing tmux/Zsh discovery hooks, so you can replay AI-generated edits even after closing the OpenCode TUI.

  Line 123: 2. When the SSE recorder drops a new patch, run `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh` to get the file path, then inside Neovim run `:DiffviewFileHistory <patch>` or open it in a scratch buffer for review.

  Line 128: `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` now covers the new surfaces:

  Line 130: - `scripts/opencode/test-sse-recorder.ts` (Bun harness) ensures the recorder plugin logs events/diffs.

  Line 131: - `scripts/opencode/test-nvim-health.sh` runs `nvim --headless` → `luafile` (module probes) → `:checkhealth opencode` → `:checkhealth codecompanion` so CI flags missing plugins immediately. Set `OPENCODE_NVIM_APPNAME` if you use a non-default Neovim profile.

  Line 132: - `scripts/opencode/diffview-latest.sh` gives tooling + tests a stable entry point for the most recent diff snapshot.

  Line 141: - Pairing this dashboard with the SSE recorder means Entire checkpoints capture both AI session history and evolving Neovim configuration history (for example, referencing the most active month when reviewing opencode usage).


/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-rotation.md:
  Line 12: 1. **Enrollment** – Fish helper `opencode-accounts add|capture` ( `.config/fish/functions/opencode-accounts.fish`) logs in, saves each provider profile under `~/.opencode/accounts/<name>/openai-auth.json`, and mirrors the same payload into Codex via `_ai_accounts_sync`. The `.accounts` file preserves round-robin order and `.current` records the pointer that last worked.

  Line 13: 2. **Preflight guardrails** – Every launcher (`gwt-ticket`, `scripts/opencode/tmux-open.sh`, cross-provider bridge, dev panes, Neovim bridge) shells through `scripts/opencode/check-and-rotate.sh` before OpenCode starts. That script runs `scripts/opencode/usage-check.sh`, invokes `opencode-accounts check-and-rotate` when needed, and refuses to launch if no usable token exists.

  Line 14: 3. **Session capture** – The OpenAI rotation plugin (`.opencode/plugins/openai-rotate.ts`) watches `chat.message` events for any OpenAI/Codex provider, snapshots the outgoing prompt (agent, system, model, tool list, prompt parts), and waits for completion metadata.

  Line 16: 5. **Observability + hygiene** – Environment flags (`OPENCODE_ROTATE_DEBUG_LOG`, `OPENCODE_USAGE_CHECK_SCRIPT`, `OPENCODE_ACCOUNTS_DIR`) keep traces, while harnesses (`scripts/opencode/test-rotation.sh`, `test-live-rotation.sh`, `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode`) and doctor scripts prove the flow end-to-end.

  Line 20: ### `opencode-accounts` (fish)

  Line 22: Path: `.config/fish/functions/opencode-accounts.fish`

  Line 24: - `add <name>` – runs `opencode auth login` and saves the resulting `.openai` entry to `~/.opencode/accounts/<name>/openai-auth.json`, updating `.accounts` and `.current` pointers.

  Line 26: - `switch <name>` – rewrites `~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json` (OpenCode’s live credential) with a saved profile.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Launch OpenCode inside tmux with alternate-screen disabled so tmux copy-mode
# retains scrollback. Restores the prior setting when OpenCode exits.
set -euo pipefail

# Determine current window id so we can scope setw calls via tmux target
WINDOW=${TMUX_PANE:-}

restore_alternate_screen() {
	if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
		tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
	else
		tmux setw -w alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
	fi
}

trap restore_alternate_screen EXIT

if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
	tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen off >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # check-and-rotate.sh - Ensure OpenCode has an active OpenAI account token
3: set -euo pipefail
4: 
5: QUIET=0
6: if [ "${1:-}" = "--quiet" ]; then
7:     QUIET=1
8:     shift
9: fi
10: 
11: log() {
12:     if [ "$QUIET" -eq 0 ]; then
13:         echo "$@"
14:     fi
15: }
16: 
17: err() {
18:     echo "$@" >&2
19: }
20: 
21: DOTFILES_ROOT="${DOTFILES_ROOT:-$HOME/dotfiles}"
22: USAGE_CHECK="${OPENCODE_USAGE_CHECK_SCRIPT:-$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/opencode/usage-check.sh}"
23: 
24: if [ ! -x "$USAGE_CHECK" ]; then
25:     log "opencode-check: usage-check script not found at $USAGE_CHECK (skipping)"
26:     exit 0
27: fi
28: 
29: if "$USAGE_CHECK" --quiet >/dev/null 2>&1; then
30:     exit 0
31: fi
32: 
33: status=$?
34: 
35: if [ "$status" -ne 1 ]; then
36:     err "opencode-check: usage probe failed (exit $status)"
37:     exit "$status"
38: fi
39: 
40: log "opencode-check: current account is rate limited; rotating..."
41: 
42: FISH_BIN="${FISH_BIN:-$(command -v fish || true)}"
43: 
44: rotate_with_fish() {
45:     "$FISH_BIN" -c 'if functions -q opencode-accounts
46:         opencode-accounts check-and-rotate
47:     else
48:         opencode auth login --provider openai
49:     end'
50: }
51: 
52: if [ -n "$FISH_BIN" ]; then
53:     if rotate_with_fish >/dev/null 2>&1; then
54:         if "$USAGE_CHECK" --quiet >/dev/null 2>&1; then
55:             log "opencode-check: rotation complete"
56:             exit 0
57:         fi
58:         err "opencode-check: usage still blocked after rotation"
59:         exit 1
60:     fi
61:     err "opencode-check: fish rotation failed; falling back to opencode auth login"
62: fi
63: 
64: if opencode auth login --provider openai >/dev/null 2>&1; then
65:     if "$USAGE_CHECK" --quiet >/dev/null 2>&1; then
66:         log "opencode-check: new login succeeded"
67:         exit 0
68:     fi
69:     err "opencode-check: usage still blocked after new login"
70:     exit 1
71: fi
72: 
73: err "opencode-check: unable to rotate account"
74: exit 1

(End of file - total 74 lines)
</content>

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # Filtered Test Runner - Run specific test groups from dotfiles test suites
3: # Usage: ./scripts/test-filter.sh [group] [--list]
4: #
5: # This is a "harness engineering" tool designed for AI agents.
6: # Instead of running full test suites, agents can run targeted test groups
7: # to get faster feedback on specific changes.
8: #
9: # Examples:
10: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh fish          # Test Fish shell config only
11: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh stow          # Test stow compatibility only
12: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh claude        # Test Claude Code config only
13: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax  # Validate setup.sh syntax only
14: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile      # Validate Brewfile structure
15: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh mcp           # Test MCP server parity
16: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh all           # Run all groups
17: #   ./scripts/test-filter.sh --list        # List available test groups
18: 
19: set -euo pipefail
20: 
21: # Colors
22: RED='\033[0;31m'
23: GREEN='\033[0;32m'
24: YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
25: BLUE='\033[0;34m'
26: NC='\033[0m'
27: 
28: SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
29: DOTFILES_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
30: 
31: TESTS_RUN=0
32: TESTS_PASSED=0
33: TESTS_FAILED=0
34: 
35: run_test() {
36:     local test_name="$1"
37:     local test_command="$2"
38:     TESTS_RUN=$((TESTS_RUN + 1))
39:     if eval "$test_command" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
40:         echo -e "${GREEN}  PASS${NC} $test_name"
41:         TESTS_PASSED=$((TESTS_PASSED + 1))
42:     else
43:         echo -e "${RED}  FAIL${NC} $test_name"
44:         TESTS_FAILED=$((TESTS_FAILED + 1))
45:     fi
46: }
47: 
48: list_groups() {

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Filtered Test Runner - Run specific test groups from dotfiles test suites
# Usage: ./scripts/test-filter.sh [group] [--list]
#
# This is a "harness engineering" tool designed for AI agents.
# Instead of running full test suites, agents can run targeted test groups
# to get faster feedback on specific changes.
#
# Examples:
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh fish          # Test Fish shell config only
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh stow          # Test stow compatibility only
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh claude        # Test Claude Code config only
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax  # Validate setup.sh syntax only
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile      # Validate Brewfile structure
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh mcp           # Test MCP server parity
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh all           # Run all groups
#   ./scripts/test-filter.sh --list        # List available test groups

set -euo pipefail

**Migration Plan**
1. Replace install source:
   - Add `tap "leohenon/tap"` and `brew "leohenon/tap/ocv"` to `homebrew/Brewfile`.
   - Remove/retire `anomalyco/tap/opencode` from Brewfile and `scripts/setup.sh`.
   - Update `scripts/setup.sh` `_install_opencode()` to install/upgrade `ocv`, but keep installing `opencode-with-claude` via `bun`.

2. Preserve the `opencode` command name:
   - Add a repo-managed shim at `scripts/bin/opencode` that execs `ocv "$@"`.
   - This keeps all existing scripts, Fish functions, tmux launchers, hooks, docs, and muscle memory working.
   - `scripts/bin` is already on Fish and Zsh PATH, so this should shadow `/opt/homebrew/bin/opencode`.

3. Keep plugin parity:
   - Keep `.config/opencode/opencode.json` as the canonical config.
   - Preserve existing configured plugins:
     - `@tarquinen/opencode-dcp@latest`
     - `opencode-pty`
     - `opencode-vibeguard`
     - `opencode-with-claude.ts`
     - `nvim-open.ts`
   - Fix the current absolute plugin paths if needed; they currently point at `/Users/shaheislam/...`, while this machine is `/Users/shahe.islam/...`.

4. Enable OCV Vim TUI ergonomics:
   - Update `.config/opencode/tui.json` for OCV-specific behavior:
     - `vim_system_clipboard_register: true`
     - `vim_enter_submit: false` so insert-mode Enter stays newline-friendly
     - `input_force_submit: "alt+return"`
     - `copy_mode: "<leader>v"`
     - `prompt_copy_selection: "<leader>y"`
   - Resolve the existing conflict where `variant_list` already uses `<leader>v`; move it to something like `<leader>V`.

5. Update launch surfaces:
   - `scripts/opencode/tmux-open.sh` can continue to call `opencode`, relying on the shim.
   - `opencode-local.fish`, `gwt-ticket.fish`, auth rotation scripts, and bridge scripts can mostly remain unchanged because the shim preserves the CLI name.
   - Where install/help text says `brew install opencode`, update it to `brew install leohenon/tap/ocv`.

6. Update diagnostics and tests:
   - `scripts/opencode/doctor.sh` should report both:
     - `opencode` shim path
     - underlying `ocv` binary/version
   - Add/adjust tests in `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` to assert:
     - Brewfile contains `leohenon/tap/ocv`
     - `scripts/bin/opencode` exists and delegates to `ocv`
     - `tui.json` has OCV Vim keybinds
     - plugin list still has all existing plugin entries.

7. Validate after implementation:
   - `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode`
   - `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile`
   - `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`
   - `scripts/test-filter.sh fish`
   - `opencode --version` should resolve to OCV through the shim.
   - `opencode-doctor --quick` should show plugin parity and binary health.

**Key Choice**
I’d replace OpenCode by preserving the `opencode` command name as a shim to `ocv`, rather than renaming every workflow to `ocv`. That gives the seamless migration you want while minimizing churn and keeping current plugins/hooks intact.

state.output:
# opencode.nvim

<https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/077daa78-d401-4b8b-98d1-9ba9f94c2330>

## ✨ Features

- Connect to _any_ `opencode`, or provide an integrated instance
- Share editor context (buffer, selection, diagnostics, etc.)
- Input prompts with completions, highlights, and normal-mode support
- Select prompts from a library and define your own
- Execute commands
- Monitor and respond to events
- View, accept or reject, and reload edits
- Interact with `opencode` via an in-process LSP
- _Vim-y_ — supports ranges and dot-repeat
- Simple, sensible defaults to get you started quickly

## 📦 Setup

### [lazy.nvim](https://github.com/folke/lazy.nvim)

```lua
{
  "nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim",
  version = "*", -- Latest stable release
  dependencies = {
    {
      -- `snacks.nvim` integration is recommended, but optional
      ---@module "snacks" <- Loads `snacks.nvim` types for configuration intellisense
      "folke/snacks.nvim",
      optional = true,
      opts = {
        input = {}, -- Enhances `ask()`
        picker = { -- Enhances `select()`
          actions = {
            opencode_send = function(...) return require("opencode").snacks_picker_send(...) end,
          },
          win = {
            input = {
              keys = {
                ["<a-a>"] = { "opencode_send", mode = { "n", "i" } },
              },
            },
          },
        },
      },
    },
  },
  config = function()
    ---@type opencode.Opts
    vim.g.opencode_opts = {
      -- Your configuration, if any; goto definition on the type or field for details
    }

    vim.o.autoread = true -- Required for `opts.events.reload`

    -- Recommended/example keymaps
    vim.keymap.set({ "n", "x" }, "<C-a>", function() require("opencode").ask("@this: ", { submit = true }) end, { desc = "Ask opencode…" })
    vim.keymap.set({ "n", "x" }, "<C-x>", function() require("opencode").select() end,                          { desc = "Execute opencode action…" })
    vim.keymap.set({ "n", "t" }, "<C-.>", function() require("opencode").toggle() end,                          { desc = "Toggle opencode" })

    vim.keymap.set({ "n", "x" }, "go",  function() return require("opencode").operator("@this ") end,        { desc = "Add range to opencode", expr = true })
    vim.keymap.set("n",          "goo", function() return require("opencode").operator("@this ") .. "_" end, { desc = "Add line to opencode", expr = true })

    vim.keymap.set("n", "<S-C-u>", function() require("opencode").command("session.half.page.up") end,   { desc = "Scroll opencode up" })
    vim.keymap.set("n", "<S-C-d>", function() require("opencode").command("session.half.page.down") end, { desc = "Scroll opencode down" })

    -- You may want these if you use the opinionated `<C-a>` and `<C-x>` keymaps above — otherwise consider `<leader>o…` (and remove terminal mode from the `toggle` keymap)
    vim.keymap.set("n", "+", "<C-a>", { desc = "Increment under cursor", noremap = true })
    vim.keymap.set("n", "-", "<C-x>", { desc = "Decrement under cursor", noremap = true })
  end,
}
```

### [nixvim](https://github.com/nix-community/nixvim)

```nix
programs.nixvim = {
  extraPlugins = [
    pkgs.vimPlugins.opencode-nvim
  ];
};
```

> [!TIP]
> Run `:checkhealth opencode` after setup.

## ⚙️ Configuration

`opencode.nvim` provides a rich and reliable default experience — see all available options and their defaults [here](./lua/opencode/config.lua).

### Contexts

`opencode.nvim` replaces placeholders in prompts with the corresponding context:

| Placeholder    | Context                                                         |
| -------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `@this`        | Operator range or visual selection if any, else cursor position |
| `@buffer`      | Current buffer                                                  |
| `@buffers`     | Open buffers                                                    |
| `@visible`     | Visible text                                                    |
| `@diagnostics` | Current buffer diagnostics                                      |
| `@quickfix`    | Quickfix list                                                   |
| `@diff`        | Git diff                                                        |
| `@marks`       | Global marks                                                    |
| `@grapple`     | [grapple.nvim](https://github.com/cbochs/grapple.nvim) tags     |

> [!TIP]
> `opencode` reads referenced files from disk — save your changes!

### Prompts

Select prompts to review, explain, and improve your code:

| Name          | Prompt                                                                 |
| ------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `diagnostics` | Explain `@diagnostics`                                                 |
| `diff`        | Review the following git diff for correctness and readability: `@diff` |
| `document`    | Add comments documenting `@this`                                       |
| `explain`     | Explain `@this` and its context                                        |
| `fix`         | Fix `@diagnostics`                                                     |
| `implement`   | Implement `@this`                                                      |
| `optimize`    | Optimize `@this` for performance and readability                       |
| `review`      | Review `@this` for correctness and readability                         |
| `test`        | Add tests for `@this`                                                  |

### Server

You can manually run `opencode`s however you like and `opencode.nvim` will find them!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> You _must_ run `opencode` with the `--port` flag to expose its server.

If `opencode.nvim` can't find an existing `opencode`, it uses the configured server to start one for you, defaulting to an embedded terminal.

#### Keymaps

`opencode.nvim` sets these normal-mode keymaps in the embedded terminal for Neovim-like message navigation:

| Keymap  | Command                  | Description           |
| ------- | ------------------------ | --------------------- |
| `<C-u>` | `session.half.page.up`   | Scroll up half page   |
| `<C-d>` | `session.half.page.down` | Scroll down half page |
| `gg`    | `session.first`          | Go to first message   |
| `G`     | `session.last`           | Go to last message    |
| `<Esc>` | `session.interrupt`      | Interrupt             |

#### Customization

Example using [`snacks.terminal`](https://github.com/folke/snacks.nvim/blob/main/docs/terminal.md) instead:

```lua
local opencode_cmd = 'opencode --port'
---@type snacks.terminal.Opts
local snacks_terminal_opts = {
  win = {
    position = 'right',
    enter = false,
    on_win = function(win)
      -- Set up keymaps and cleanup for an arbitrary terminal
      require('opencode.terminal').setup(win.win)
    end,
  },
}
---@type opencode.Opts
vim.g.opencode_opts = {
  server = {
    start = function()
      require('snacks.terminal').open(opencode_cmd, snacks_terminal_opts)
    end,
    stop = function()
      require('snacks.terminal').get(opencode_cmd, snacks_terminal_opts):close()
    end,
    toggle = function()
      require('snacks.terminal').toggle(opencode_cmd, snacks_terminal_opts)
    end,
  },
}
```

## 🚀 Usage

### Ask — `require("opencode").ask()`

Input a prompt for `opencode`.

- Press `<Up>` to browse recent asks.
- Highlights and completes contexts and `opencode` subagents.
  - Press `<Tab>` to trigger built-in completion.
- End the prompt with a space to append instead of submit.
- When using `snacks.input`, offers completions via in-process LSP.

### Select — `require("opencode").select()`

Select from all `opencode.nvim` functionality.

- Prompts
- Commands
- Server controls

Highlights and previews items when using `snacks.picker`.

### Prompt — `require("opencode").prompt()`

Prompt `opencode`.

- Injects configured contexts.
- `opencode` will interpret references to files or subagents.

### Operator — `require("opencode").operator()`

Wraps `prompt` as an operator, supporting ranges and dot-repeat.

### Command — `require("opencode").command()`

Command `opencode`:

| Command                  | Description                                        |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `session.list`           | List sessions                                      |
| `session.new`            | Start a new session                                |
| `session.select`         | Select a session                                   |
| `session.share`          | Share the current session                          |
| `session.interrupt`      | Interrupt the current session                      |
| `session.compact`        | Compact the current session (reduce context size)  |
| `session.page.up`        | Scroll messages up by one page                     |
| `session.page.down`      | Scroll messages down by one page                   |
| `session.half.page.up`   | Scroll messages up by half a page                  |
| `session.half.page.down` | Scroll messages down by half a page                |
| `session.first`          | Jump to the first message in the session           |
| `session.last`           | Jump to the last message in the session            |
| `session.undo`           | Undo the last action in the current session        |
| `session.redo`           | Redo the last undone action in the current session |
| `prompt.submit`          | Submit the TUI input                               |
| `prompt.clear`           | Clear the TUI input                                |
| `agent.cycle`            | Cycle the selected agent                           |

### LSP

> [!WARNING]
> This feature is experimental! Try it out with `vim.g.opencode_opts.lsp.enabled = true`.

`opencode.nvim` provides an in-process LSP to interact with `opencode` via the LSP functions you're used to!

| LSP Function | `opencode.nvim` Handler                                                |
| ------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Hover        | Asks `opencode` for a brief explanation of the symbol under the cursor |
| Code Actions | Asks `opencode` to explain or fix diagnostics under the cursor         |

## 👀 Events

`opencode.nvim` forwards `opencode`'s Server-Sent-Events as an `OpencodeEvent` autocmd:

```lua
-- Handle `opencode` events
vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("User", {
  pattern = "OpencodeEvent:*", -- Optionally filter event types
  callback = function(args)
    ---@type opencode.server.Event
    local event = args.data.event
    ---@type number
    local port = args.data.port

    -- See the available event types and their properties
    vim.notify(vim.inspect(event))
    -- Do something useful
    if event.type == "session.idle" then
      vim.notify("`opencode` finished responding")
    end
  end,
})
```

### Edits

When `opencode` edits a file, `opencode.nvim` automatically reloads the corresponding buffer.

### Permissions

When `opencode` requests a permission, `opencode.nvim` waits for idle to ask you to approve or deny it.

#### Edits

For edit requests, `opencode.nvim` opens the target file in a new tab and uses Neovim's `:diffpatch` to display the proposed changes side-by-side. See `:h 'diffopt'` for customization.

| Keymap  | Function                                                                      |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `da`    | Accept the entire edit request                                                |
| `dr`    | Reject the entire edit request                                                |
| `]c/[c` | Next/prev change                                                              |
| `dp`    | Natively accept _only_ the hunk under the cursor, and reject the edit request |
| `do`    | Natively reject _only_ the hunk under the cursor, and reject the edit request |
| `q`     | Close the diff                                                                |

### Statusline

```lua
require("lualine").setup({
  sections = {
    lualine_z = {
      {
        require("opencode").statusline,
      },
    }
  }
})
```

## 🙏 Acknowledgments

- Inspired by [nvim-aider](https://github.com/GeorgesAlkhouri/nvim-aider), [neopencode.nvim](https://github.com/loukotal/neopencode.nvim), and [sidekick.nvim](https://github.com/folke/sidekick.nvim).
- Uses `opencode`'s TUI for simplicity — see [sudo-tee/opencode.nvim](https://github.com/sudo-tee/opencode.nvim) for a Neovim frontend.
- [mcp-neovim-server](https://github.com/bigcodegen/mcp-neovim-server) may better suit you, but it lacks customization and tool calls are slow and unreliable.

-- opencode.nvim - AI coding agent integration
-- Connects to opencode server (running with --port) via HTTP + SSE
-- Shares editor context (buffers, selections, diagnostics) with the agent

local function ask_with_context(prefix, submit)
  return function()
    require("opencode").ask(prefix, { submit = submit or false })
  end
end

local function run_command(command)
  return function()
    require("opencode").command(command)
  end
end

  {
    "nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim",
    version = "*",

state.title: ../neovim/lua/plugins/opencode.lua

parts.text:
`opencode.nvim` is the Neovim-side integration layer. `opencode-vim`/`ocv` is the terminal binary replacement.

They should work together like this:

| Layer | Tool | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Terminal OpenCode | `leohenon/opencode-vim` / `ocv` | Replaces the OpenCode TUI with Vim motions, copy mode, Vim registers, better prompt editing |
| Command compatibility | `scripts/bin/opencode` shim | Keeps every existing workflow calling `opencode`, but runs `ocv` underneath |
| Neovim integration | `nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim` | Sends Neovim context to OpenCode: `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, visual selections, operator ranges |
| Existing parity layer | `.config/opencode/opencode.json` + `.opencode/plugins/*` | Keeps DCP, PTY, VibeGuard, Claude compat, account rotation, nvim-open, hooks |

Important finding: `opencode.nvim` is already configured in `~/neovim/lua/plugins/opencode.lua`.

So the plan changes slightly:

1. Replace the `opencode` binary with `ocv`, but keep the command name via shim.
2. Let `opencode.nvim` continue invoking `opencode`.
3. Because `opencode` will resolve to our shim, Neovim automatically uses `ocv`.
4. Keep `.config/opencode/opencode.json` and all existing plugins unchanged except path fixes.
5. Validate that `ocv` still supports the server mode `opencode.nvim` needs: `opencode --port`.
6. Keep `opencode.nvim` as the editor-native workflow:
   - `<leader>aoa` asks with `@this`
   - `go` uses operator/range context
   - named prompts handle explain/fix/review/test/diff
   - events reload buffers and update statusline
7. Keep `ocv` as the terminal-native workflow:
   - tmux pane
   - standalone terminal sessions
   - copy mode
   - Vim motions inside the TUI

Net: `ocv` makes the terminal TUI Vim-capable; `opencode.nvim` makes OpenCode understand Neovim context. We should use both.
Make this change
I’ll implement the `ocv` replacement now while preserving the `opencode` command surface, then run the targeted dotfiles test groups that cover setup, Brewfile, Fish, and OpenCode parity.

			((SKIP++)) || true
		fi
	else
		print_warning "Codex not available (need codex binary + auth via 'codex login') - skipping"
		((SKIP++)) || true
	fi

	# Test: OpenCode provider (if available)
	# Requires: opencode binary + Ollama running + model pulled
	has_opencode=false
	opencode_model="${CROSS_PROVIDER_OPENCODE_MODEL:-ollama/qwen2.5-coder:1.5b}"
	if command -v opencode &>/dev/null && curl -sf http://localhost:11434/api/tags &>/dev/null; then
		has_opencode=true
		print_info "Running cross-provider bridge with OpenCode ($opencode_model)..."

parts.text:
**Updating installation details**

I need to update the doctor tests documentation and handle permissions, specifically using chmod. Before that, I'll check if the parent directory exists with a command like ls. Once I confirm that, I can proceed with patching the doctor tests. I want to ensure I add an ocv binary check after opening the code, and possibly label widths will be fine. If the path is scripts/bin, I could mark it as PASS. I'll also include test-filter lines and consider updating the opencode-nvim documentation with ocv. Finally, I think targeted grep for brew installations after patching might be necessary.

input.patchText:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/scripts/opencode/doctor.sh
@@
 if command -v opencode >/dev/null 2>&1; then
 	print_result PASS "opencode binary" "$(command -v opencode)"
 else
 	print_result FAIL "opencode binary" "OpenCode is not installed or not on PATH"
 fi

+if command -v ocv >/dev/null 2>&1; then

+	if [ -n "$ocv_version" ]; then

+	else
+		print_result PASS "ocv binary" "$(command -v ocv)"
+	fi
+else

+fi

input.patchText:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-nvim.md
@@
-OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
+OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane. The terminal binary is `ocv` from [`leohenon/opencode-vim`](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim), exposed through the repo-managed `scripts/bin/opencode` shim so existing launchers keep working while the TUI gains Vim motions, copy mode, and clipboard-register support. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin is the complementary Neovim integration layer: it keeps the same backend assistant but embeds editor context directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
@@
-- The backend assistant is still OpenCode, so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
+- The backend assistant is still OpenCode-compatible (`opencode` resolves to `ocv`), so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
@@
 5. Continue to use the tmux pane when you need a dedicated transcript view or when Neovim is not open—both entry points can coexist because they talk to the same OpenCode backend.
+
+## opencode-vim and opencode.nvim together
+
+- `ocv` replaces the terminal TUI and provides full Vim-mode ergonomics when OpenCode runs in tmux or a standalone shell.
+- `scripts/bin/opencode` preserves the command name expected by `gwt-ticket`, tmux launchers, auth rotation, cross-provider hooks, and `opencode.nvim`.
+- `opencode.nvim` keeps using `opencode --port`; the shim routes that to `ocv --port`, so editor-native prompts inherit the same binary and plugin stack.
+- `.config/opencode/tui.json` owns TUI Vim settings such as system clipboard register sync, prompt scrolling, copy mode, and force-submit bindings.
*** End Patch

metadata.diff:
Index: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-nvim.md
===================================================================
--- /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-nvim.md
+++ /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/docs/opencode-nvim.md
@@ -1,7 +1,7 @@
 # opencode.nvim vs tmux Side Pane
 
-OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
+OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane. The terminal binary is `ocv` from [`leohenon/opencode-vim`](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim), exposed through the repo-managed `scripts/bin/opencode` shim so existing launchers keep working while the TUI gains Vim motions, copy mode, and clipboard-register support. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin is the complementary Neovim integration layer: it keeps the same backend assistant but embeds editor context directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
 
 ## Why the plugin is a material upgrade
 
 - **Zero-copy context** – prompts can reference placeholders such as `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, or `@visible`. The plugin resolves those against your current buffer/selection before sending them to OpenCode, so you stop yanking snippets into the side pane.
@@ -12,9 +12,9 @@
 - **Session UX** – the plugin auto-starts/stops OpenCode servers, exposes commands (`session.select`, `session.interrupt`, etc.) through pickers, and includes optional Snacks integrations. Launching a pane simply spawns `opencode` and leaves lifecycle management to you.
 
 ## What stays the same
 
-- The backend assistant is still OpenCode, so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
+- The backend assistant is still OpenCode-compatible (`opencode` resolves to `ocv`), so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
 - You can connect the plugin to any running OpenCode instance via the `server.port` option, which means the tmux binding can keep a long-lived side pane if you want a dedicated transcript while Neovim handles buffer-aware asks.
 - Permissions and hooks still flow through the existing `.opencode/plugins/claude-compat.ts` stack—`opencode.nvim` just surfaces them in-editor.
 
 ## Comparison snapshot
@@ -36,8 +36,15 @@
 3. Reuse the recommended keymaps (`<C-a>` ask, `<C-x>` select, `go` operator) or map them under `<leader>o` to avoid conflicts with tmux bindings.
 4. Run `:checkhealth opencode` after wiring it up; the check validates that OpenCode is discoverable and events are flowing.
 5. Continue to use the tmux pane when you need a dedicated transcript view or when Neovim is not open—both entry points can coexist because they talk to the same OpenCode backend.
 
+## opencode-vim and opencode.nvim together
+
+- `ocv` replaces the terminal TUI and provides full Vim-mode ergonomics when OpenCode runs in tmux or a standalone shell.
+- `scripts/bin/opencode` preserves the command name expected by `gwt-ticket`, tmux launchers, auth rotation, cross-provider hooks, and `opencode.nvim`.
+- `opencode.nvim` keeps using `opencode --port`; the shim routes that to `ocv --port`, so editor-native prompts inherit the same binary and plugin stack.
+- `.config/opencode/tui.json` owns TUI Vim settings such as system clipboard register sync, prompt scrolling, copy mode, and force-submit bindings.
+
 By moving repetitive prompt + context work into Neovim we remove most of the friction highlighted in this ticket while leaving the tmux bindings available for workflows outside the editor.
 
 ## Hybrid workflow with CodeCompanion

Before that, I think it’s a good idea to update the setup. I also suspect there might be a duplication issue in the Brewfile due to an existing Homebrew tap. I should ensure that the `opencode` command is in the current PATH, potentially checking my bash or zsh configurations later.

`brew install leohenon/tap/ocv` hit a GitHub release download `502`, so the repository wiring is correct but the bottle fetch failed. I’m adding a Bun package fallback in setup and using it now so the machine still gets `ocv` today.

content.summary:
User asked: “Can we move over seamlessly to https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim so we can use vim capabiltiies fully with opencode”. Initial inspection found dotfiles OpenCode config at `.config/opencode/opencode.json`, docs at `docs/opencode-nvim.md`, OpenCode setup in `scripts/setup.sh`, Brewfile entries for `anomalyco/tap/opencode`, Fish functions such as `.config/fish/functions/opencode-local.fish`, `.config/fish/functions/opencode-doctor.fish`, and the existing Neovim bridge/plugin stack. WebFetch of `leohenon/opencode-vim` showed it is a fork of OpenCode exposed as `ocv`, installable via `brew install leohenon/tap/ocv`, with built-in Vim motions, copy mode, prompt config in `tui.json`, `vim_enter_submit`, `vim_system_clipboard_register`, `prompt_max_height`, and keybinds like `copy_mode` and `prompt_copy_selection`. The assistant initially proposed wiring `ocv` alongside existing OpenCode. User clarified: “No i want you to replace opencode with it, make a plan for that, we should maintain parity though and keep all existing plugins”. A migration plan was produced: replace Homebrew source with `leohenon/tap/ocv`, keep the `opencode` command via a repo-managed `scripts/bin/opencode` shim to `ocv`, preserve `.config/opencode/opencode.json` and all plugins (`@tarquinen/opencode-dcp@latest`, `opencode-pty`, `opencode-vibeguard`, local `opencode-with-claude.ts`, local `nvim-open.ts`), add OCV Vim TUI settings, update launch surfaces/help text/doctor/tests, and validate with `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode`, `brewfile`, `setup-syntax`, and `fish`. User then asked about `https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim`. WebFetch of its README showed it is complementary Neovim integration, not a terminal binary replacement: it connects to any `opencode` or starts one with `opencode --port`, shares editor context placeholders (`@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, etc.), provides ask/select/operator commands, events, permissions, diff accept/reject, statusline, and optional LSP. Inspection of `/Users/shahe.islam/neovim/lua/plugins/opencode.lua` showed `nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim` is already configured in the separate `~/neovim` repo with keymaps under `<leader>ao*`, `go`/`goo` operators, prompts for explain/fix/review/test/document/optimize/implement/diff/diagnostics, events enabled with reload/permissions, LSP enabled, and status tracked via `vim.g.opencode_status`. The recommendation was to use both: `ocv` for Vim-capable terminal TUI, `opencode.nvim` for editor-native context, both using the preserved `opencode` command shim.

User then said “Make this change” and build mode was enabled. Implementation performed via `apply_patch` only for file edits:
- `homebrew/Brewfile`: replaced the old tap/comment `anomalyco/tap` with `tap "leohenon/tap"`; replaced `brew "anomalyco/tap/opencode"` with `brew "leohenon/tap/ocv"  # OpenCode fork with Vim mode; exposed as opencode via scripts/bin shim`.
- `scripts/setup.sh`: updated `_install_opencode()` to tap `leohenon/tap`, install/upgrade `leohenon/tap/ocv`, report `ocv installed/upgraded/already latest`, and print `opencode compatibility shim uses ocv` when `ocv` exists. Also added a Bun fallback after Homebrew failure: if `ocv` is still missing and `bun` exists, run `bun add -g @leohenon/ocv@latest`, printing `ocv installed via bun fallback` or warning on failure. Existing `bun add -g opencode-with-claude@latest` remains preserved.
- `.config/opencode/opencode.json`: fixed stale absolute plugin paths from `/Users/shaheislam/...` to `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.config/opencode/plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts` and `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.config/opencode/plugin/nvim-open.ts`.
- `.config/opencode/tui.json`: added OCV Vim settings: `vim_system_clipboard_register: true`, `vim_enter_submit: false`, `prompt_max_height: 35`, `prompt_scrollbar: true`. Added keybinds `copy_mode: "<leader>v"`, `prompt_copy_selection: "<leader>y"`, `input_force_submit: "alt+return"`; moved `variant_list` from `<leader>v` to `<leader>V` to avoid copy-mode conflict.
- Added new executable-intended file `scripts/bin/opencode` containing a Bash shim: `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, `set -euo pipefail`, checks `command -v ocv`, prints `opencode shim: ocv is not installed. Run: brew install leohenon/tap/ocv` and exits `127` if missing, else `exec ocv "$@"`.
- `.config/fish/functions/opencode-local.fish`: updated install hint from `brew install opencode` to `brew install leohenon/tap/ocv`.
- `scripts/setup-selfhost-llm.sh`: updated install hint from `brew install opencode` to `brew install leohenon/tap/ocv`.
- `.config/fish/functions/gwt-ticket.fish`: updated OpenCode missing error to `Install: brew install leohenon/tap/ocv`.
- `scripts/opencode/doctor.sh`: after the existing `opencode binary` check, added an `ocv binary` check with `ocv --version` when available; if missing, fail with `Install with: brew install leohenon/tap/ocv`.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh` OpenCode test section: added assertions for `tap "leohenon/tap"`, `brew "leohenon/tap/ocv"`, removal of `anomalyco/tap/opencode`, existence of `scripts/bin/opencode`, shim delegation to `exec ocv "$@"`, OCV TUI settings (`vim_system_clipboard_register`, `vim_enter_submit`, `keybinds.input_force_submit`, `copy_mode` and `variant_list` conflict avoidance), and plugin parity checks for `opencode-with-claude` and `nvim-open` in addition to existing DCP/PTY/VibeGuard checks.
- `docs/opencode-nvim.md`: updated the intro to explain that the terminal binary is `ocv` from `leohenon/opencode-vim`, exposed through `scripts/bin/opencode`, while `nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim` is the complementary Neovim integration. Updated “What stays the same” to say `opencode` resolves to `ocv`. Added a new section “opencode-vim and opencode.nvim together” explaining `ocv` replaces the TUI, `scripts/bin/opencode` preserves command compatibility for `gwt-ticket`, tmux launchers, auth rotation, cross-provider hooks, and `opencode.nvim`, `opencode.nvim` continues to use `opencode --port` which routes to `ocv --port`, and `.config/opencode/tui.json` owns the Vim TUI settings.

After edits, ran `chmod +x "scripts/bin/opencode"` successfully. Attempted `brew tap leohenon/tap && brew install leohenon/tap/ocv`; Homebrew auto-updated and tapped `leohenon/tap`, but install failed while downloading the release asset `https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim/releases/download/v1.14.29-ocv.3.30/ocv-darwin-arm64.zip` with curl error 56 / HTTP 502. This caused the assistant to add the Bun fallback in `scripts/setup.sh`. Next active steps: run `bun add -g @leohenon/ocv@latest` to install OCV on this machine, verify `command -v opencode`, `command -v ocv`, `opencode --version`, and then run targeted validation: `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode`, `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile`, `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`, `scripts/test-filter.sh fish`, plus potentially `opencode-doctor --quick`. Need inspect/fix any failures. Do not commit unless user explicitly asks.

parts.text:
**Troubleshooting package manager setup**

I'm dealing with an issue related to the CodeArtifact registry failing, so I'm considering using the default npm registry with a command like `bun add -g --registry https://registry.npmjs.org @leohenon/ocv@latest`. I need to check if the package exists first.  
  
Then there's a matter of ensuring that the fish shell configuration has the scripts/bin directory appropriately added to the PATH, specifically after Homebrew. I'll need to look into fish_add_path and figure out its behavior regarding appending or prepending paths as well. In zsh, I should check the relevant .zshrc as well.

The Bun fallback hit your work CodeArtifact registry (`401`), so I’m retrying against the public npm registry explicitly. I’m also checking PATH precedence because the old Homebrew `opencode` can still shadow the new shim if `scripts/bin` is not early enough.

parts.text:
**Checking command outputs**

I realize that in bash, I should avoid using `echo` if it's not necessary, but in fish, I need to check the command output. I'll also look into whether the script located in `scripts/bin` is not in the PATH, which might be related to the fish configuration that includes `paths.fish`. I need to dive into that config and grep through `paths.fish` to see what's going on.

Two issues surfaced: Bun installed the package but blocked its postinstall, so the `ocv` binary is currently a stub; and `opencode` still resolves to the old Homebrew binary in Fish. I’m fixing both rather than leaving a partial migration.

run_test ".gitignore carve-out for settings.json" "grep -q '!\.entire/settings\.json' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.gitignore'"

    # Phase 2: Enhanced checkpoints.fish subcommands
    run_test "checkpoints.fish has attribution case" "grep -q 'case attribution' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/checkpoints.fish'"
    run_test "checkpoints.fish has generate case" "grep -q 'case generate' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/checkpoints.fish'"
    run_test "checkpoints.fish has explain case" "grep -q 'case explain' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/checkpoints.fish'"

    # Phase 3: Attribution bindings in FZF browsers
    run_test "Commit browser has ALT-A attribution" "grep -q 'alt-a:change-preview' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/git-fzf-actions.fish'"
    run_test "Checkpoint browser has ALT-A attribution" "grep -c 'alt-a:change-preview' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/git-fzf-actions.fish' | grep -q '2'"

    # Phase 4: Multi-agent support in gwt-ticket
    run_test "gwt-ticket supports --ckpt-agent" "grep -q 'ckpt-agent' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/gwt-ticket.fish'"
    run_test "gwt-ticket passes --agent to entire" "grep -q '\-\-agent.*ckpt_agent' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/gwt-ticket.fish'"

    # Phase 5: Checkpoint browser uses entire rewind --list
    run_test "Checkpoint browser tries entire rewind --list" "grep -q 'entire rewind --list' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/.config/fish/functions/git-fzf-actions.fish'"

    # Phase 6: Documentation
    run_test "CLAUDE.md documents ckpt-agent flag" "grep -q 'ckpt-agent' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/CLAUDE.md'"

parts.text:
**Considering patching setup**

I’m thinking about patching the paths in my setup and considering the bun fallback registry and trust. After adding bun, there's a chance the postinstall might be blocked. I’m wondering if I should add the command `bun pm trust -g @leohenon/ocv` but am unsure if `-g` is accepted for trust since it didn't show in the help section. I need to test it out, maybe running it in a global package. I’ll proceed cautiously to avoid destructive issues.

parts.text:
**Troubleshooting brew issues**

It seems like my brew process timed out, or maybe it's still running. I should check if it's truly terminated. I'll also want to verify if the necessary components are installed. It might be good to review the formula I need too. Perhaps I should consider checking the raw branch master as well? I’ll need to read the formula and possibly fetch the necessary repo from the web. Let's figure this out!

parts.text:
**Evaluating script order**

I’m figuring out the right order for some scripts, specifically with the ocv and .bun files. It seems like I should place the scripts first in the Fish PATH, then maybe the .bun file after that. Right now, /opt is the third in line, and if brew ocv exists, it probably takes precedence. However, the setup should really check for `ocv --version` to avoid issues if brew isn’t available.

**Revising command checks**

I'm examining the use of `ocv --version` to ensure the command exists and runs correctly. It seems that if the command is broken, it will fail, suggesting I need a better way to handle this by iterating through possible paths. If the `ocv` command fails but exists, I could print a message indicating it's broken. However, I shouldn't run a version check each time for performance reasons. I wonder if the setup should try to update to brew if necessary and consider trust in the installation process.

**Handling upgrades and installation**

After I run `bun add`, I'll execute `bun pm trust -g @leohenon/ocv` and then verify the installation. If it fails, I’ll need to warn the user. I realize I might also need to use the `--registry` option. There’s a possibility that an old, broken version of Bun is already installed. If `command_exists ocv` returns true during setup, it may lead to an upgrade process. I wonder how to handle cases where the `brew upgrade` command doesn't have the necessary formula installed.

# Ensure fifc Tab binding takes precedence over autopair.fish
# This rebinds Tab to the fifc/git/docker wrapper after all plugins have loaded
if functions -q _fifc_or_fzf
    bind \t _fifc_or_fzf
    bind -M insert \t _fifc_or_fzf
end

# Mise shims for version-managed tools (must be early in PATH)
fish_add_path $HOME/.local/share/mise/shims

# K9s kubectl-node-shell plugin
fish_add_path $HOME/dotfiles/scripts

# Krew kubectl plugin manager (with KREW_ROOT support)
# PERF: Use fish_add_path for dedup (raw set -gx PATH appends duplicates on each shell)
if set -q KREW_ROOT
    fish_add_path $KREW_ROOT/.krew/bin
else
    fish_add_path $HOME/.krew/bin

I'll do a sequential process: `brew uninstall opencode && brew install leohenon/tap/ocv` and set a timeout of 600 seconds. If the uninstall goes well, but the install fails, it looks like the opencode command could find a shim but not have ocv. Alright, let's go for it!

The old `opencode` Homebrew formula is still installed, so I’m removing it now and installing `ocv` from the new tap. This is required so the `opencode` command resolves through the compatibility shim instead of the stale binary.

parts.text:
**Inspecting hook harness issue**

I’m noticing that the entire hook harness seems to be failing, and this might be tied to something preexisting. I really need to inspect it closely and run the script to see what’s going on. If the harness is the cause of the problem, I’ll need to fix it to ensure everything works smoothly again. So, it’s time to dive in and see what’s failing with that harness!

Most targeted checks passed, but `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` exposed one failing pre-existing OpenCode hook harness. I’m running that harness directly to see whether the OCV change caused it or whether the test expectation is stale.

metadata.preview:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/../.." && pwd)"
PLUGIN="$ROOT/.opencode/plugins/entire.ts"

if ! command -v bun >/dev/null 2>&1; then
	echo "FAIL bun is required for Entire hook validation" >&2
	exit 1
fi

if [ ! -f "$PLUGIN" ]; then
	echo "FAIL missing plugin: $PLUGIN" >&2
	exit 1
fi

TMPDIR="$(mktemp -d)"
trap 'rm -rf "$TMPDIR"' EXIT

HARNESS="$TMPDIR/harness.mjs"
